description: This bar chart presents the corrosion rates of Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels (LDSs) after 30 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comparing four samples with different thermomechanical processing histories. The x-axis labels denote: S0 (hot-rolled and water-quenched without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C for 2 h), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150°C for 2 h). The y-axis quantifies corrosion rate in mm/y. The measured values reveal a non-monotonic trend with solution treatment temperature: S0 exhibits the highest corrosion rate at 0.326 mm/y, while S950 shows the minimum at 0.160 mm/y. The corrosion rate increases again with higher solution treatment temperatures, reaching 0.248 mm/y for S1050 and 0.281 mm/y for S1150. Error bars indicate experimental variability. This data supports the core conclusion that grain size optimization via solution treatment at 950°C (yielding ~8.7 μm equiaxed grains with high grain boundary density) significantly enhances corrosion resistance by facilitating the rapid formation of a dense, continuous Al-rich oxide film (90.17 at% Al, 25.2 nm thick). In contrast, the coarse-grained S1150 (32 μm) with lower grain boundary density exhibits reduced corrosion resistance due to discontinuous oxide film formation with higher defect densities and lower Al content (~69.91 at%).
